problem.title: Linked List Cycle
problem.description:
Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it.
                
Definition: A cycle exists if there is some node in the list that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer.
assumptions: Return the node where the cycle begins, or null.

example.explanation: There is a cycle in the linked list, where the tail connects to the 1st node (0-indexed).

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Floyd's Cycle Finding Algorithm (Two Pointers) [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Use two pointers: slow advances one step at a time and fast advances two steps. If a cycle exists, fast will eventually lap slow and they will meet inside the cycle. If fast reaches null, the list is acyclic. This runs in O(n) time and O(1) space with no extra data structures.
solutions.code_python:
# class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.next=None
from typing import Optional

def hasCycle(head: Optional[object]) -> bool:
    slow = head
    fast = head
    while fast is not None and fast.next is not None:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:
            return True
    return False

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Hash Set Approach [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Use a hash set to track visited nodes.
solutions.code_python:
# class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.next=None
from typing import Optional

def hasCycleHashSet(head: Optional[object]) -> bool:
    visited: set = set()
    current = head
    while current is not None:
        if current in visited:
            return True
        visited.add(current)
        current = current.next
    return False
